ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Tom Yum hotpot soup base; closest parent is generic soup broth food product.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Soups and broths) is the closest parent category; no specific Tom Yum hotpot base code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Tom Yum hotpot soup base.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical hotpot serving of Tom Yum soup base is approximately 400 g, representing the broth consumed or used as a cooking medium during a single hotpot meal.

nutrient_source.title: Tom Yum Soup (clear, with shrimp) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table; adapted for soup base without solid ingredients
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for a standalone Tom Yum hotpot soup base. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for Tom Yum soup (clear broth variant, NDB 06972 / SR Legacy) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for Thai tom yum soup. Solid protein ingredients (shrimp, mushrooms) are excluded to reflect a soup-base-only product; macros are adjusted downward accordingly. Sodium is retained at broth level. This is an estimated adaptation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of soups and broths — analogous estimate based on clear vegetable/spiced broth; University of Sydney GI Database reference for low-carbohydrate soups
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tom Yum hotpot soup base. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous clear spiced broth / vegetable soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently show low GI (range 25–45) for clear soups with minimal starch content. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (35 × 2.2 × 4) / 100 = 3.1 g, classified as low GL. gi_category set to 'low' per ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per 400 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Tom Yum hotpot soup base consumption or request a reduced-sodium version, as a single 400 g serving can contribute approximately 1680 mg sodium — over 70% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low carbohydrate content (~2.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tom Yum hotpot soup base has a low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate load, making it a suitable broth choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the overall meal glycemic impact depends on the solid ingredients added to the hotpot.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium and moderate saturated fat from shrimp paste and fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content of Tom Yum soup base poses a cardiovascular risk for individuals with heart disease; those with cardiovascular disease should consume this broth in moderation and pair it with low-sodium, high-fibre hotpot ingredients to mitigate risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Yum Hotpot Soup Base is a spicy and sour Thai-inspired broth used as the cooking medium in hotpot (steamboat) dining. It is prepared by slow-simmering aromatic herbs and spices including lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, fresh chilli, and tomatoes, with umami depth provided by fish sauce and shrimp paste. The resulting broth is characteristically tangy, fragrant, and moderately spicy, with a clear to lightly cloudy appearance. In the hotpot context, diners cook raw meats, seafood, vegetables, tofu, and noodles directly in the simmering broth, which absorbs additional flavour from the ingredients over time. Nutritionally, the soup base itself is low in calories (~18 kcal per 100 g), low in fat, and low in carbohydrates, giving it a low glycemic index of approximately 35. However, it is notably high in sodium (~420 mg per 100 g), which is a significant clinical consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The broth contains bioactive compounds from lemongrass and galangal that have been studied for anti-inflammatory properties. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the broth itself poses minimal glycemic risk, though the overall meal impact depends on the ingredients cooked in it. This dish is served at Flavours by Saute (Funan, Singapore) and is representative of the Thai-influenced hotpot cuisine popular in Singapore's food scene.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists for a standalone Tom Yum hotpot soup base in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy Tom Yum soup entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA Thai soup data, with solid ingredients removed to reflect a broth-only product. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database clear soup entries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated/adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Tom Yum soup, clear broth) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Thai soup composition data; no exact standalone Tom Yum hotpot soup base entry was found in any approved primary source. 2. Solid hotpot ingredients (shrimp, mushrooms, tofu, noodles) are excluded from these per-100g values, which reflect the broth/soup base only. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this broth product. 4. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this broth product. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this broth product. 6. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this broth product. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this broth product. 8. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy broth data; no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional Tom Yum preparation. 9. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database clear spiced soup/broth entries; no direct GI measurement for Tom Yum hotpot soup base was found. gi_category set to 'low' per ≤55 threshold rule. 10. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Mixed' as this is a soup broth product, not a grain or staple. 11. Cuisine is classified as Thai (origin of Tom Yum) with Singapore restaurant context; multilingual names include Thai script per mandatory Thai cuisine rule, plus Singaporean multilingual set (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given the Singapore restaurant context. 12. Halal status is assumed based on typical restaurant preparation; kosher status is not confirmed. 13. The input food_name 'HOTPOT Tom Yummy' is a menu-style promotional name; canonical English name has been normalised to 'Tom Yum Hotpot Soup Base'.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft built. Identified that food_group 'Grains and staples' was incorrect for a soup base; reclassified to 'Mixed'.
Iteration 1: Normalised food_name from promotional 'HOTPOT Tom Yummy' to canonical 'Tom Yum Hotpot Soup Base'.
Iteration 1: Added Thai script local name (mandatory for Thai cuisine) and full Singaporean multilingual set (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) for Singapore restaurant context.
Iteration 1: Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes (vitamin_b12, folate, vitamin_d, vitamin_e, selenium).
Iteration 1: Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency — all strings match verbatim.

Iteration 2: Verified GI category threshold (GI=35 ≤ 55 → 'low' confirmed). Verified trans_fat_g=0.0 documented as confirmed zero. Verified all match_type values are within allowed set. Final self-score: 8/8, all hard gates pass → PASS.
